fact: June 1 is exactly 152 days into the year, which means there are exactly 213 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, June 1 marks the beginning of meteorological summer, when temperatures start getting seriously hot and sticky.
Fireflies typically begin their magical light shows around this time in North America, blinking their butts to find mates in the warm evening air.
The sun usually rises before 6 AM in most northern places on June 1, giving you extra morning light to catch bugs or ride your bike.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, June 1 kicks off winter, so kids are bundling up while American kids are getting ready for beach weather.
Rose petals are often at their most fragrant around June 1 in many places, releasing sweet smells that can make you sneeze if you stick your nose too close.
Baby birds are typically learning to fly around this time, which means you might see clumsy fledglings crash-landing in your yard.
The summer solstice is only 20 days away from June 1, so daylight is stretching longer and longer each day like a rubber band.
Mosquitoes often reach peak annoyance levels in early June across much of North America, thanks to warm temperatures and spring rain creating perfect breeding puddles.
In northern Alaska, the sun barely sets at all around June 1, giving kids nearly 24 hours of daylight to play outside.
Tree pollen counts typically drop significantly by June 1 in most places, which means fewer people walking around with red, watery eyes and constant sniffling.
Ocean water temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are usually warming up enough by June 1 that swimming doesn't feel like jumping into a giant ice cube.
